author: @Marcos-Zap
text:
I am not an engineering, not even close, actually a drop out, but I have this crazy idea of a self contained power generator that I think it is as close as it can get to perpetual movement. Please someone help me figure it out, lets all assume IT DOESN'T WORK so save your negative comment, but if you think it could, what do you think it could prevent the water from flowing and how to overcome those challenges, thanks!!...Here it is:
We have two tanks of water, tank 1 is at the bottom and is full of water, tank 2 is 10 ft above and is half full of water. two pipes (A & B), pipe A is connected to the bottom of tank 1,  and the middle of tank 2 (just below the water level), with a valve (A-1) at the entry of tank 1, pipe B is connected at the top of tank 1 and the bottom on tank 2, with a valve B-1 at the entry of tank 1, tank 2 is half full of water and half full of air but it's air tight with one valve (C) at the top of tank 2 (to be able to fill with water at the level desired, half way and just enough to submerge the entry of pipe A), with both valves (A-1 and B-1) closed, with open the valve C of tank 2 and fill with water (leaving the desired air space), when closed, tank 2 is air tight, pipes A & B are full of water and we open both valves A-1 and B-1. the water will start flow downward on pipe B (tank 2 will have enough gallons of water for is gravity to want to flow downward) forcing the water in tank 2 to want to lower but since no air enters to replace the space leaving the tank, the pipe A will begin to pull the water upward (due to the air pressure inside) Similar to us sipping water upward with a straw from a glass of water.. This way, the water flows from tank 1 to pipe A to tank 2 to pipe B to tank 1. Add a hydro turbine at the bottom of pipe B and we got our self a self contained power generator. 
Any feedback? Thanks!

author: @barryfrost6720
text:
Rob, it would be interesting to see the effect of laminar flow versus turbulent flow of the incoming water on the overall efficiency of the turbine. To pick up off of another person’s comment, the turbines could be placed in series.  How close one turbine is to another would be limited by the turbulence.
Another factor that would affect efficiency would be the water exiting the turbine. If one doesn’t give enough space to the exiting water, turbulence will in essence back up and interfere with the rotation of the turbine blades. I am imagining a series of inverted bell shaped housings that give enough room for the water being flung outwards by one turbine to then funnel it into the next.
This is cool stuff!!! Keep up the good work and happy exploring!

author: @heleneandneilcampbell7408
text:
There is a massive blind spot in the design of the squirrel cage blower except for the Mike Waters turbine.
The squirrel cage was developed from earlier simple centrifugal pumps and when it was realised the tips did all the work the squirrel cage was born. But when is a vane a wing? Look at a squirrel cage from the wing perspective and it will be seen that the low pressure above the curve of the wing is on the high pressure side of the pump. It's wings are upside down! The late Peter Rollo filed a NZ provisional patent in 1995 showing an average 50% improvement in performance over three traditional blowers. As far as I can ascertain no full patent was ever filed. In Mike Waters' turbine the airpressure is reversed and is therefore correct. He agrees with me. Robert, there is another factor making the Waters turbine super efficient, the downstream square end causes suction on the vanes.
There are a lot of great ideas lurking undeveloped in the patent files, your tinkering Robert is very important.
Neil Campbell

author: @PetraKann
text:
The point of the Water Vortex Turbine generator is that the water vortex is present in the water flow, irrespective of whether you have a turbine inserted in the water flow or not.
It's distinctly different from say a water wheel which is also gravity driven by falling water in primarily one direction.
A vortex is also driven by gravity as well as the Coriolis effect (a weak force on this scale, and is actually controversial as to whether it determines the direction of the vortex. The direction of large cyclones/hurricanes is determined by the Coriolis effect being different in the northern and southern hemispheres).

The flow of water from the bucket onto the turbine in your demonstration actually slows the turbine down the longer it spends in the spinning part of the turbine. That is, the fastest exit for the water when it strikes the top cone will be in a straight line so that it will impart the maximum energy onto the blades as quickly as possible. 
IT can easily work as you have clearly demonstrated but optimising this turbine set up is very tricky indeed.

Cheers
Keep up the good work. You engage with your viewers very well.

author: @douglascox9996
text:
Multi-cylinder steam engines of a century ago reused the depleted residual energy of steam released from the initial high pressure cylinder, by feeding it into the next cylinder. The second cylinder was increased in size to take advantage of the even further depleted steam heat and pressure. Similarly, the steam released from the second cylinder was fed into a third, even larger cylinder to take advantage of the third level of depleted steam heat and pressure. All pistons in these cylinders were connected to the same crankshaft to drive the engine.

All that was by way of explaining the principle of using the depleted energy of water released by the first turbine to feed into the next larger turbine, then from that into the next even larger turbine, all connected to the same central drive shaft to drive a larger generator.

author: @RocketIIIman
text: excuse the probably rambling question, how do you increase the power from a generator? presumably you are measuring an open circuit voltage most times, and when you place a load on it an amount of current is drawn, which acts to hold back the turbine, so if you are drawing 10W from a turbine this would be the same as applying 10W of friction to the system, so at some point the turbine will be spinning as fast as it can go but can you increase the electrical friction to pull more power from the driving force? can you have additional open circuit coils that are then switched in to increase the current you can pull from the system?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you can't increase the power from a generator mate because generators don't produce power - they convert energy from one form to another - for example - here the power originates in the water - to get more power you need more water or more energy in the water - like increasing the head - the generator will convert that to electrical energy - you can get a better efficiency that's for sure - but basically that's where the limit comes from - so to improve power out you need to improve conversion efficiency - more coils doesn't improve efficiency

author: @screen-protector
text:
I was thinking about another idea way back. Here in Scotland is a lot of rain. So, if we'd take an IBC let's say a 1000 litres, and plug in the room drainage through it to fill up to the brim.  Only than over pass this IBC to not overflood it if the stream would be too strong.

Now, on the outlet, create one after another small turbines in series, and use a gravity to get the water down. As we can't gain the energy, we know it won't produce more Watts than we've used to get that water to the top. But, as we didn't put this water, it was a rain which did it, we've gained the wattage out of it ;). 

The question only is what is the optimal capacitance of the IBC in total to make it worth tinkering ;). I'd say for a small house, 1kW might be enough for a whole day, but for a family of 4 with no EV, this might not be enough for 1 hour. 

IMHO, if we can't produce per hour 1kW of energy, these projects won't have too much impact at the end. That should be our minimal goal to achieve :).

author: @the.original.throwback
text: Another inspiring video.  Thanks.  Your foray into hydrodynamic turbine design is similar to a long used turbine called the "Francis runner" design, but in reverse.  The Francis runner is surrounded by a structure called the scroll case which directs incoming water in a spiral around the runner to apply equal pressure and flow into the runner blades from the outside, spinning the runner, with the water then discharging down through the center of the runner into a chamber called the draft tube, and then on its merry way.  There are various and quite different styles of hydro turbine designs, each specialized to be most efficient for the pressure and velocity of the incoming water, called "head pressure" which, in turn, is usually dependent on the vertical distance the water drops through a pipe or penstock feeding the turbine.  Thanks again, and keep inspiring future engineers to continue exploring and inventing.

author: @HostileRespite
text: If this is the generator you made a few episodes back with the S-coil and magnets right under the fan disc, you'll want to reconsider the design for use underwater. Electric motors underwater need to be contained in a watertight compartment. There are a few reasons for this, electrocution being the primary concern, but also rendering a loss in useable power, and corrosion of the metals when a current is applied to them. If I recall the design and understand the concept of this pipe assembly correctly, the magnets in this design would be completely exposed to water, depending on how much water is expected to flow through the pipe. If this is intended for a rain gutter, then a simple angled rain skirting around the edge will probably do the trick. Put one on the fan disk and one on the S-coil disk angled up to help prevent splashback. If you wanted it to sit in a fully submerged pipe though... it'll fry.

replies.author: @ArtisticImpressionsbyBobRouth

replies.author: @SchwaAlien
replies.text: They do this in British Columbia, in the greater Vancouver area our water comes from mountain reservoirs and at the point where the pressure needs to be reduced for residential units they have installed generators rather than just letting the excess pressure be wasted.  The electricity is used to power main road traffic lights independently of the grid so that they continue working indefinitely during a power outage.  Theoretically each home could have their own pressure regulator that also generates some electricity during use, but it would not be enough on it’s own for much, but could help feed a battery charger or something like that...
